glossary.def: An ensemble method that builds an additive model one weak learner at a time, each new tree fit to the negative gradient of the loss with respect to the current predictions. For squared-error loss that negative gradient is simply the residual y − F, so 'fit the gradient' reduces to 'fit the leftover error'; changing the loss changes only the residual formula, not the machinery.

The negative gradient of the loss at each training point, −∂L/∂F evaluated at the current model, which gradient boosting regresses on instead of the raw labels. It is the steepest-descent direction in function space; for squared loss it equals the ordinary residual, for log-loss it is the label minus the predicted probability.

The factor ν ∈ (0,1] multiplying each new tree before it is added to the ensemble: F_m = F_{m−1} + ν·h_m. Smaller ν takes gentler steps, requiring more trees to fit but generalizing better; ν and the number of trees trade off, roughly, as a fixed product.

The original boosting algorithm (1997): it reweights training examples so that misclassified ones grow heavier each round, and votes the weak learners by α = ½·ln((1−ε)/ε). It is exactly forward stagewise additive modeling under the exponential loss exp(−yF), which also makes it sensitive to mislabeled points and outliers.

XGBoost's use of both the gradient g and the Hessian h of the loss in a second-order Taylor expansion of the objective. The closed-form optimal leaf value is w* = −G/(H+λ) and a split's gain is the children's structure scores minus the parent's, less a per-leaf cost γ — letting the model weight each example by how curved the loss is there.

LightGBM's trick of pre-binning each feature into a fixed number of buckets (default 255) and accumulating per-bin gradient and Hessian histograms, so finding the best split costs O(#bins·d) instead of O(n·d). Histogram subtraction (a child's histogram = parent − sibling) halves the work again; the cost is a slightly coarser grid of candidate cut points.

Growing a tree by always splitting the single leaf with the highest gain anywhere in the tree (best-first), as LightGBM does, versus XGBoost's level-wise growth that splits a whole depth before going deeper. Leaf-wise reaches a lower training loss for the same leaf budget but overfits more, so it is capped with num_leaves well below 2^max_depth.

CatBoost's leakage-free way of encoding a categorical value by the smoothed mean target of only the rows that precede it in a random permutation: (Σ_{j<i} y_j + a·p)/(count + a). Because a row's own label never enters its own encoding, the target cannot leak; the same prefix-ordering idea, applied to residuals, is 'ordered boosting'.

refs.cite: Friedman, J. H. (2001). Greedy Function Approximation: A Gradient Boosting Machine. Annals of Statistics 29(5).
Freund, Y. & Schapire, R. E. (1997). A Decision-Theoretic Generalization of On-Line Learning and an Application to Boosting. Journal of Computer and System Sciences 55(1).
Friedman, J., Hastie, T. & Tibshirani, R. (2000). Additive Logistic Regression: A Statistical View of Boosting. Annals of Statistics 28(2).
Chen, T. & Guestrin, C. (2016). XGBoost: A Scalable Tree Boosting System. KDD 2016.
Ke, G., Meng, Q., Finley, T., Wang, T., Chen, W., Ma, W., Ye, Q. & Liu, T.-Y. (2017). LightGBM: A Highly Efficient Gradient Boosting Decision Tree. NeurIPS 2017.
Prokhorenkova, L., Gusev, G., Vorobev, A., Dorogush, A. V. & Gulin, A. (2018). CatBoost: Unbiased Boosting with Categorical Features. NeurIPS 2018.
Grinsztajn, L., Oyallon, E. & Varoquaux, G. (2022). Why Do Tree-Based Models Still Outperform Deep Learning on Typical Tabular Data? NeurIPS 2022 Datasets & Benchmarks.
